ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for lion's mane mushroom curry rice. FOODON_00002809 (curry dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (rice-based dish) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for Nanyang-style mushroom curry rice with mixed grains.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Nanyang Curry Lion's Mane Mushroom Rice is approximately 350 g, comprising a portion of mixed grain rice (red rice, brown rice, quinoa) topped with lion's mane mushroom in Nanyang-style curry sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database (mixed grain rice, brown rice, red rice, quinoa) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (lion's mane mushroom, curry sauce components). Weighted composite per dish composition.
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 55% mixed grain rice (red rice, brown rice, quinoa blend), 25% lion's mane mushroom (cooked), 15% Nanyang curry sauce (coconut milk base), 5% vegetable oil and aromatics. HPB Foodo data used for rice components; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 11267 mushrooms cooked, NDB 11165 coconut milk) used for mushroom and sauce components. Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for this dish category.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed grain rice (red rice, brown rice, quinoa blend) estimated from analogous foods: Brown rice GI 50 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Am J Clin Nutr); Red rice GI 55 (Foster-Powell et al. 2002, Am J Clin Nutr); Quinoa GI 53 (Vega-Gálvez et al. 2010). Weighted composite GI estimated at 52 for the mixed grain base; curry sauce and mushroom components have negligible glycemic contribution.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nanyang Curry Lion's Mane Mushroom Rice. GI of 52 is estimated as a weighted average of the mixed grain rice base (brown rice GI 50, red rice GI 55, quinoa GI 53) per University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. The curry sauce and lion's mane mushroom components contribute minimal glycemic load. GL per serving calculated as (52 × 21.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 39.1, rounded to 39.3 accounting for serving size of 350 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a full 350 g serving delivers approximately 1085 mg sodium, representing 47% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume this dish in moderation and request reduced-salt curry sauce preparation; the mixed grain base and lion's mane mushroom are inherently low in sodium, so the primary sodium source is the curry sauce and seasoning.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Mixed grain rice base (red rice, brown rice, quinoa) provides a lower GI of approximately 52 compared to white rice (GI 72–83), with dietary fibre at 2.4 g per 100 g supporting slower glucose absorption; however, total carbohydrate per serving (approximately 75 g) requires portion control.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may benefit from the lower glycemic index of the mixed grain base relative to white rice, but should limit portion size to half a standard serving and monitor postprandial glucose, as the total glycemic load per full serving remains high at approximately 39.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content of approximately 2.8 g per 100 g (9.8 g per serving) from coconut milk in the curry sauce; lion's mane mushroom contributes beta-glucan fibre with potential LDL-lowering properties, partially offsetting cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be aware of the saturated fat contribution from coconut milk in the curry sauce; choosing a smaller portion and requesting a lighter sauce preparation can reduce saturated fat intake, while the lion's mane mushroom and mixed grain components provide beneficial dietary fibre.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nanyang Curry Lion's Mane Mushroom Rice is a contemporary Singaporean fusion dish served at health-conscious cafes and modern Asian restaurants such as Flavours by Saute at Funan Mall, Singapore. The dish features lion's mane mushroom (Hericium erinaceus), a prized edible fungus with a seafood-like texture and mild, slightly sweet flavour, served atop a proprietary blend of red rice, brown rice, and quinoa. This mixed grain base is nutritionally superior to plain white rice, offering higher dietary fibre (approximately 2.4 g per 100 g), a lower glycemic index of approximately 52, and additional protein from quinoa. The Nanyang-style curry sauce is a Southeast Asian coconut milk-based curry, fragrant with turmeric, cumin, coriander, and chilli, reflecting the multicultural culinary heritage of Singapore and the Nanyang (South Seas) region. The dish is typically vegetarian and halal-friendly. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g serving provides approximately 518 kcal, 14.7 g protein, 17.9 g fat, and 75.3 g carbohydrate. The lion's mane mushroom contributes beta-glucan polysaccharides associated with immune modulation and potential LDL cholesterol reduction. The dish is clinically relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its lower GI mixed grain base, though portion control remains important given the total glycemic load. Sodium content from the curry sauce warrants attention for individuals with hypertension. This dish represents the growing trend of functional and wellness-oriented rice bowls in Singapore's food service sector.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using HPB Singapore Foodo data for rice components and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for mushroom and curry sauce components. GI is estimated from analogous foods in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and grounded in approved sources, but the composite values have not been laboratory-verified for this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated composites; no direct laboratory analysis of this specific dish is available from an approved source. Values should be treated as indicative for the dish category. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats; confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy data for component ingredients. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): dish is plant-based (mushroom and grain base with coconut milk); no animal-derived cholesterol-containing ingredients. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): dish is plant-based; lion's mane mushroom and plant ingredients contain no vitamin B12 by nature. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source data for the component ingredients at the level of specificity required. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source data for mixed grain rice components and is not reliably estimated for this composite dish. 7. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate weighted from brown rice (GI 50), red rice (GI 55), and quinoa (GI 53) per University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 8. Dietary flags: is_vegan set to false as coconut milk may contain trace dairy cross-contamination in some preparations; is_vegetarian set to true as the dish contains no meat or fish. 9. Serving size of 350 g is an estimate based on standard Singapore restaurant rice bowl portions; actual serving size may vary by restaurant. 10. Ontology codes use closest_parent matches as no exact codes exist for this specific dish in FoodOn or FoodEx2.
